key_insight: A grain-growth model trained on synthetic data can generalize to out-of-distribution microstructures, and a boundary-masked attention design further improves accuracy by focusing on grain-boundary pixels.
engineering_relevance: This suggests AI-driven microstructure evolution predictors can be made more robust for real materials workflows without retraining, especially when physics-guided attention aligns with boundary-driven growth behavior.
method: Evaluated a previously trained deep learning grain-growth predictor on three OOD cases: experimental microstructures, bimodal grain-size distributions, and abnormal grain growth.
Compared a baseline model against a physics-informed boundary-masked attention variant without retraining or fine-tuning.
Analyzed attention maps to check whether the model concentrated on grain boundaries in a physically meaningful way.
evidence_signal: On bimodal microstructures, the boundary-masked model improved SSIM from 0.6221 to 0.7609 and reduced mean grain-size error from 8.75% to 3.57% across OOD evaluation.
watch_item: The gains appear strongest when the test morphology remains close to the training domain, so robustness may be weaker for more severe distribution shifts or unseen boundary patterns.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Attention Mechanism and Generalization Capability of Deep Learning-Based Grain Growth Evolution Prediction
